--- title: "Logistic BHM - RStan v JAGS v Nimble - 1" output: rmarkdown::html_vignette: toc: true toc_depth: 2 number_sections: true vignette: > %\VignetteIndexEntry{Two-arm trial TFP v RStan} %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown} %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8} --- ## Quick start This vignette compares the same Bayesian hierarchical model fitted first with Rstan and then with Vegas, and is similar the previous vignette for a logistic regression but extened to a basket trial design scenario with two baskets. Only key snippets and outputs are shown below. The full vignette is available [here](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/GSK-Biostatistics/vignettes-asia/refs/heads/main/vegas/stanvegas2_jax.qmd). For the equivalent vignette but which uses Apple's MLX framework to run the computation's on a GPU see [here](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/GSK-Biostatistics/vignettes-asia/refs/heads/main/vegas/stanvegas2_mlx.qmd). A GPU can offer a considerable reduction in computational time. ### Model Formulation The model implemented here is a non-centered parameterization for a Basket trial with two baskets and a logistic response model. $$ \begin{aligned} \mu_0 &\sim \text{Normal}(0, 2.5)\\ \sigma_0 &\sim \text{Half-Normal}(0, 2.5) \\ \mu_1 &\sim \text{Normal}(0, 2.5) \\ \sigma_1 &\sim \text{Half-Normal}(0, 2.5) \\ \tilde{\theta}_j &\sim \text{Normal}(0, 1)\quad\enspace\text{for}\enspace{j=1,\dots,4} \\ \beta_{0,0} &= \mu_0 + \sigma_0 \tilde{\theta}_1 \quad\quad\hspace{0.2cm}\text{ intercept - log odds control basket 0}\\ \beta_{0,1} &= \mu_0 + \sigma_0 \tilde{\theta}_2 \quad\quad\hspace{0.2cm}\text{ intercept - log odds control basket 1}\\ \beta_{1,0} &= \mu_1 + \sigma_1 \tilde{\theta}_3 \quad\quad\hspace{0.2cm}\text{ treatment - log OR basket 0}\\ \beta_{1,1} &= \mu_1 + \sigma_1 \tilde{\theta}_4 \quad\quad\hspace{0.2cm}\text{ treatment - log OR basket 1}\\ \text{logit(}{p_i}) &= \beta_0 + \beta_1 z_i\quad\quad\enspace\enspace\text{for}\enspace{i=1,\dots,N} \\ y_i &\sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_i) \end{aligned} $$ ## Data set The R chunk below creates a simple dataset of three cols: - a binary response variable y (0/1 = non-responder/responder), - a (dummy) basket ID variable (=1) - a binary treatment variable (0/1 = control/test treatment) The basket ID is currently set fixed at 1, denoting there is only one basket in this trial, i.e. a classical two arm randomized trial design. Baskets will be added in other vignettes. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ### Prepare model inputs set.seed(99999) # Set up data rr_k_ctrl <- c(0.20,0.25) # control response rate for each basket rr_k_trt <- c(0.40,0.45) # treatment response rate for each basket K<-length(rr_k_ctrl) # number of baskets N_k_ctrl <- rep(50, K) # number of control participants per basket N_k_trt <- rep(50, K) # number of treatment participants per basket N_k <- N_k_ctrl + N_k_trt # number of participants per basket (both arms combined) N <- sum(N_k) # total sample size k_vec <- rep(1:K, N_k) # N x 1 vector of basket indicators (1 to K) z_vec<-NULL; y<-NULL; for(i in 1:K){ # for each basket repeat 0-control 1-trt according to the specifc Ns z_vec<-c(z_vec,rep(0:1,c(N_k_ctrl[i],N_k_trt[i]))) # treatment/control indicator y<-c(y, c(rbinom(N_k_ctrl[i],1,rr_k_ctrl[i]), # bernoulli for control rbinom(N_k_trt[i],1,rr_k_trt[i]))) # for trt } thedata<-data.frame(y,basketID=k_vec,Treatment=z_vec) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} my_table1_object <- head(thedata) %>% kable() %>% kable_styling(full_width = F) %>% column_spec(1, bold = F)#, color = "blue") #> Warning: 'xfun::attr()' is deprecated. #> Use 'xfun::attr2()' instead. #> See help("Deprecated") #> Warning: 'xfun::attr()' is deprecated. #> Use 'xfun::attr2()' instead. #> See help("Deprecated") my_table1_object ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
y basketID Treatment
0 1 0
1 1 0
0 1 0
1 1 0
0 1 0
0 1 0
````` ::: ::: ## RStan The code for implementing the above model is RStan is given below. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ### Stan model BHM_stan_1 <- "data { int K; // number of baskets (K >= 2) int N; // total number of participants int N_k[K]; // K x 1 vector of basket sample sizes //int N_k; int k_vec[N]; // N x 1 vector of basket indicators int z_vec[N]; // N x 1 vector of treatment indicators for active treatment arm int y[N]; // N x 1 vector of binary responses } parameters { matrix[K,2] beta_tr; // K x 2 matrix of transformed regression coefficients real mu1; // scalar of hierarchical mean (log odds ratio) real sigma1; // scalar of hierarchical SD (log odds ratio) real mu0; // scalar of hierarchical mean (log odds ratio) real sigma0; // scalar of hierarchical SD (log odds ratio) } transformed parameters { matrix[K,2] beta; // K x 2 matrix of regression coefficients (without transformation) // Calculate beta coefs using transformed betas (leads to better mixing) for (k in 1:K){ beta[k,1] = mu0 + sigma0 * beta_tr[k,1]; beta[k,2] = mu1 + sigma1 * beta_tr[k,2]; } } model { mu0 ~ normal(0, 2.5); // vectorized normal priors for hierarchical means (specify SD in normal distn) sigma0 ~ normal(0, 2.5); // vectorized half-normal priors for hierarchical SDs (specify SD in normal distn) //sigma0 ~ exponential(1); //sigma0 ~ cauchy(0, 5); //sigma0 ~ gamma(2, 0.1); mu1 ~ normal(0, 2.5); // vectorized normal priors for hierarchical means (specify SD in normal distn) sigma1 ~ normal(0, 2.5); // vectorized half-normal priors for hierarchical SDs (specify SD in normal distn) //sigma1 ~ exponential(1); //sigma1 ~ cauchy(0, 5); //sigma1 ~ gamma(2, 0.1); beta_tr[,1] ~ normal(0, 1); // vectorized normal prior for the log odds for the control arm beta_tr[,2] ~ normal(0, 1); // vectorized normal prior for the log odds ratios for (i in 1:N) y[i] ~ bernoulli_logit(beta[k_vec[i], 1] + beta[k_vec[i], 2] * z_vec[i]); } " ``` ::: ### Diagnostics ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Log posterior lp_fit_2 <- log_posterior(stan_fit_2) head(lp_fit_2) #> Chain Iteration Value #> 1 1 1 -133.5735 #> 2 1 2 -131.0906 #> 3 1 3 -132.8610 #> 4 1 4 -132.1165 #> 5 1 5 -131.9547 #> 6 1 6 -129.6844 #Nuts patterns np_fit_2 <- nuts_params(stan_fit_2) head(np_fit_2) #> Chain Iteration Parameter Value #> 1 1 1 accept_stat__ 0.84973530 #> 2 1 2 accept_stat__ 0.86034269 #> 3 1 3 accept_stat__ 0.05760393 #> 4 1 4 accept_stat__ 0.66809424 #> 5 1 5 accept_stat__ 0.77369738 #> 6 1 6 accept_stat__ 0.97023867 #mcmc parcoord color_scheme_set("darkgray") posterior_fit_2 <- as.array(stan_fit_2) mcmc_parcoord(posterior_fit_2, np = np_fit_2) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](stan_jags_nimble2_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-2-1.png){width=672} ::: ```{.r .cell-code} fit_summary <- summary(stan_fit_2)$summary # View the Rhat column for all parameters print(fit_summary[, c("mean", "Rhat")]) #> mean Rhat #> mu0 -0.8012458 1.0076828 #> sigma0 0.9330413 1.0072098 #> mu1 0.6256955 1.0013423 #> sigma1 1.0105141 1.0002866 #> beta[1,1] -0.9611574 0.9999888 #> beta[1,2] 0.8325958 1.0000292 #> beta[2,1] -0.8388560 0.9999962 #> beta[2,2] 0.5518193 1.0000710 #> beta_tr[1,1] -0.1472424 1.0007267 #> beta_tr[1,2] 0.2162354 1.0002857 #> beta_tr[2,1] 0.0470609 1.0010038 #> beta_tr[2,2] -0.1438148 1.0002310 #> lp__ -133.5216196 1.0008350 ``` ::: ### Summarize results ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(bayesplot) color_scheme_set("viridisA") p<-mcmc_trace(stan_fit_2,"mu0") #ggsave("precomputed/vg2_traceplot1_stan.png", p) print(p) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](stan_jags_nimble2_files/figure-html/stanresults-1.png){width=100%} ::: ```{.r .cell-code} plot(stan_fit_2) #> 'pars' not specified. Showing first 10 parameters by default. #> ci_level: 0.8 (80% intervals) #> outer_level: 0.95 (95% intervals) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](stan_jags_nimble2_files/figure-html/stanresults-2.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ### JAGS ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} BHM_jags_2 <- " model { for (i in 1:N) { p[i] <- ilogit(beta[k_vec[i], 1] + beta[k_vec[i], 2] * z_vec[i]) y[i] ~ dbern(p[i]) } for (k in 1:K) { beta_tr[k, 1] ~ dnorm(0, 1) beta_tr[k, 2] ~ dnorm(0, 1) } for (j in 1:K){ beta[j,1] <- mu0 + sigma0 * beta_tr[j,1] beta[j,2] <- mu1 + sigma1 * beta_tr[j,2] } #tau0 <- 1 / (sigma0^2) #tau1 <- 1 / (sigma1^2) tau_mu <- 1 / (2.5^2) mu0 ~ dnorm(0, tau_mu) mu1 ~ dnorm(0, tau_mu) sigma0 ~ dunif(0, 3) sigma1 ~ dunif(0, 3) } " bhm_jags_mod <- jags.model( textConnection(BHM_jags_2), data = data_input_1, n.chains = 4, n.adapt = 10000 ) #> Warning in jags.model(textConnection(BHM_jags_2), data = data_input_1, n.chains #> = 4, : Unused variable "N_k" in data #> Compiling model graph #> Resolving undeclared variables #> Allocating nodes #> Graph information: #> Observed stochastic nodes: 200 #> Unobserved stochastic nodes: 8 #> Total graph size: 637 #> #> Initializing model update(bhm_jags_mod, 10000) jags_mod_sim = coda.samples(model = bhm_jags_mod, variable.names = c("mu0", "sigma0","mu1", "sigma1", "beta","beta_tr"), n.iter = 10000, thin =1) summary(jags_mod_sim) #> #> Iterations = 20001:30000 #> Thinning interval = 1 #> Number of chains = 4 #> Sample size per chain = 10000 #> #> 1. Empirical mean and standard deviation for each variable, #> plus standard error of the mean: #> #> Mean SD Naive SE Time-series SE #> beta[1,1] -0.96606 0.2875 0.001438 0.002836 #> beta[2,1] -0.82964 0.2765 0.001383 0.002617 #> beta[1,2] 0.83966 0.3868 0.001934 0.003737 #> beta[2,2] 0.53726 0.3786 0.001893 0.003859 #> beta_tr[1,1] -0.16736 0.7702 0.003851 0.017721 #> beta_tr[2,1] 0.02972 0.7719 0.003860 0.018188 #> beta_tr[1,2] 0.24542 0.7880 0.003940 0.015694 #> beta_tr[2,2] -0.13723 0.7915 0.003958 0.016543 #> mu0 -0.80437 0.6794 0.003397 0.028443 #> mu1 0.61417 0.7912 0.003956 0.027597 #> sigma0 0.87491 0.7653 0.003826 0.019082 #> sigma1 0.97256 0.7891 0.003946 0.017279 #> #> 2. Quantiles for each variable: #> #> 2.5% 25% 50% 75% 97.5% #> beta[1,1] -1.56370 -1.1485 -0.95496 -0.7705 -0.4316 #> beta[2,1] -1.36748 -1.0157 -0.83022 -0.6434 -0.2820 #> beta[1,2] 0.11367 0.5767 0.82610 1.0913 1.6324 #> beta[2,2] -0.22537 0.2860 0.54391 0.7959 1.2615 #> beta_tr[1,1] -1.69465 -0.6680 -0.16335 0.3252 1.3788 #> beta_tr[2,1] -1.46584 -0.4798 0.02121 0.5166 1.6122 #> beta_tr[1,2] -1.31541 -0.2682 0.24182 0.7572 1.8175 #> beta_tr[2,2] -1.72172 -0.6465 -0.12986 0.3803 1.3965 #> mu0 -2.17095 -1.1545 -0.85173 -0.5192 0.8325 #> mu1 -1.18182 0.2447 0.64917 1.0285 2.2279 #> sigma0 0.02349 0.2511 0.62842 1.3392 2.7162 #> sigma1 0.03131 0.3152 0.75835 1.4773 2.7876 #plot(jags_mod_sim) mat_jags <- as.matrix(jags_mod_sim) beta_jags <- mat_jags[, "beta[1,1]"] ``` ::: ### NIMBLE with default sampler ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} nimble_code_1 <- nimbleCode({ for (i in 1:N) { p[i] <- ilogit(beta[k_vec[i], 1] + beta[k_vec[i], 2] * z_vec[i]) y[i] ~ dbern(p[i]) } for (k in 1:K) { beta_tr[k, 1] ~ dnorm(0, 1) beta_tr[k, 2] ~ dnorm(0, 1) } for (j in 1:K){ beta[j,1] <- mu0 + sigma0 * beta_tr[j,1] beta[j,2] <- mu1 + sigma1 * beta_tr[j,2] } #tau0 <- 1 / (sigma0^2) #tau1 <- 1 / (sigma1^2) tau_mu <- 1 / (2.5^2) mu0 ~ dnorm(0, tau_mu) mu1 ~ dnorm(0, tau_mu) sigma0 ~ dunif(0, 3) sigma1 ~ dunif(0, 3) } ) #Initial values K_val <- data_input_1$K N_val <- data_input_1$N inits <- list( mu0 = 0, mu1 = 0, sigma0 = 1, sigma1 = 1, beta_tr = matrix(0, nrow = K_val, ncol = 2), beta = matrix(0, nrow = K_val, ncol = 2) ) # Build model model <- nimbleModel( nimble_code_1, # Ensure k_vec is in constants, not data constants = list( N = N_val, K = K_val, k_vec = data_input_1$k_vec, z_vec = data_input_1$z_vec ), data = list( y = data_input_1$y ), inits = inits, dimensions = list( beta_tr = c(K_val, 2), beta = c(K_val, 2), k_vec = c(N_val) ) ) #> Defining model #> Building model #> Setting data and initial values #> Running calculate on model #> [Note] Any error reports that follow may simply reflect missing values in model variables. #> Checking model sizes and dimensions #Configure the default sampler # NIMBLE automatically detects conjugacy (Gibbs) and assigns # Adaptive Metropolis to non-conjugate parameters. mcmcConf <- configureMCMC(model) #> ===== Monitors ===== #> thin = 1: beta_tr, mu0, mu1, sigma0, sigma1 #> ===== Samplers ===== #> RW sampler (8) #> - beta_tr[] (4 elements) #> - sigma0 #> - sigma1 #> - mu0 #> - mu1 mcmcConf$addMonitors("beta") #> thin = 1: beta, beta_tr, mu0, mu1, sigma0, sigma1 #conf$addMonitors(c("mu0", "mu1", "sigma0", "sigma1", "beta_tr", "beta")) #Build MCMC mcmc <- buildMCMC(mcmcConf) # Compile Cmodel <- compileNimble(model) #> Compiling #> [Note] This may take a minute. #> [Note] Use 'showCompilerOutput = TRUE' to see C++ compilation details. # Build and compile HMC sampler Cmcmc <- compileNimble(mcmc, project = model) #> Compiling #> [Note] This may take a minute. #> [Note] Use 'showCompilerOutput = TRUE' to see C++ compilation details. # Run the sampler samples <- runMCMC( Cmcmc, niter = 20000, nburnin = 5000, thin = 10, nchains = 4, samplesAsCodaMCMC = TRUE ) #> running chain 1... #> |-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------| #> |-------------------------------------------------------| #> running chain 2... #> |-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------| #> |-------------------------------------------------------| #> running chain 3... #> |-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------| #> |-------------------------------------------------------| #> running chain 4... #> |-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------| #> |-------------------------------------------------------| # Diagnosis library(coda) #plot(samples) summary(samples) #> #> Iterations = 1:1500 #> Thinning interval = 1 #> Number of chains = 4 #> Sample size per chain = 1500 #> #> 1. Empirical mean and standard deviation for each variable, #> plus standard error of the mean: #> #> Mean SD Naive SE Time-series SE #> beta[1, 1] -0.96565 0.2819 0.003639 0.004483 #> beta[2, 1] -0.83124 0.2786 0.003597 0.004499 #> beta[1, 2] 0.84102 0.3789 0.004892 0.006358 #> beta[2, 2] 0.53727 0.3810 0.004918 0.005941 #> beta_tr[1, 1] -0.23271 0.8059 0.010404 0.040014 #> beta_tr[2, 1] -0.05741 0.8036 0.010374 0.041788 #> beta_tr[1, 2] 0.23896 0.7816 0.010090 0.022329 #> beta_tr[2, 2] -0.12607 0.7910 0.010212 0.024082 #> mu0 -0.67609 0.8829 0.011398 0.078746 #> mu1 0.60871 0.7939 0.010249 0.041402 #> sigma0 0.94231 0.8199 0.010585 0.040188 #> sigma1 0.97980 0.7865 0.010153 0.023050 #> #> 2. Quantiles for each variable: #> #> 2.5% 25% 50% 75% 97.5% #> beta[1, 1] -1.55090 -1.1492 -0.95525 -0.7726 -0.4340 #> beta[2, 1] -1.39974 -1.0164 -0.83332 -0.6468 -0.2968 #> beta[1, 2] 0.13917 0.5800 0.83622 1.0910 1.6058 #> beta[2, 2] -0.23411 0.2904 0.54982 0.7959 1.2651 #> beta_tr[1, 1] -1.74201 -0.8065 -0.24444 0.2786 1.4257 #> beta_tr[2, 1] -1.50631 -0.6280 -0.06136 0.4658 1.6108 #> beta_tr[1, 2] -1.33202 -0.2485 0.23609 0.7580 1.7524 #> beta_tr[2, 2] -1.72451 -0.6358 -0.10214 0.4000 1.3896 #> mu0 -2.32053 -1.1080 -0.79650 -0.3744 1.6145 #> mu1 -1.15665 0.2310 0.62888 1.0030 2.3895 #> sigma0 0.02698 0.2623 0.67739 1.4662 2.8060 #> sigma1 0.03108 0.3202 0.77404 1.4844 2.7903 gelman.diag(samples) #> Potential scale reduction factors: #> #> Point est. Upper C.I. #> beta[1, 1] 1.00 1.00 #> beta[2, 1] 1.00 1.01 #> beta[1, 2] 1.00 1.00 #> beta[2, 2] 1.00 1.00 #> beta_tr[1, 1] 1.00 1.01 #> beta_tr[2, 1] 1.00 1.01 #> beta_tr[1, 2] 1.01 1.02 #> beta_tr[2, 2] 1.01 1.02 #> mu0 1.04 1.09 #> mu1 1.05 1.09 #> sigma0 1.01 1.03 #> sigma1 1.02 1.05 #> #> Multivariate psrf #> #> 1.03 effectiveSize(samples) #> beta[1, 1] beta[2, 1] beta[1, 2] beta[2, 2] beta_tr[1, 1] #> 4036.9493 3854.4286 3751.8302 4114.7973 528.8824 #> beta_tr[2, 1] beta_tr[1, 2] beta_tr[2, 2] mu0 mu1 #> 508.5663 1347.3179 1354.9461 181.2629 492.6602 #> sigma0 sigma1 #> 519.4346 1362.2801 all_samples <- do.call(rbind, samples) beta_nimble1 <- all_samples[, "beta[1, 1]"] ``` ::: ### NIMBLE with HMC ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} nimble_code_1 <- nimbleCode({ for (i in 1:N) { p[i] <- ilogit(beta[k_vec[i], 1] + beta[k_vec[i], 2] * z_vec[i]) y[i] ~ dbern(p[i]) } for (k in 1:K) { beta_tr[k, 1] ~ dnorm(0, 1) beta_tr[k, 2] ~ dnorm(0, 1) } for (j in 1:K){ beta[j,1] <- mu0 + sigma0 * beta_tr[j,1] beta[j,2] <- mu1 + sigma1 * beta_tr[j,2] } #tau0 <- 1 / (sigma0^2) #tau1 <- 1 / (sigma1^2) tau_mu <- 1 / (2.5^2) mu0 ~ dnorm(0, tau_mu) mu1 ~ dnorm(0, tau_mu) sigma0 ~ dunif(0, 3) sigma1 ~ dunif(0, 3) } ) #Initial values K_val <- data_input_1$K N_val <- data_input_1$N inits <- list( mu0 = 0, mu1 = 0, sigma0 = 1, sigma1 = 1, beta_tr = matrix(0, nrow = K_val, ncol = 2), beta = matrix(0, nrow = K_val, ncol = 2) ) # Build model model <- nimbleModel( nimble_code_1, # Ensure k_vec is in constants, not data constants = list( N = N_val, K = K_val, k_vec = data_input_1$k_vec, z_vec = data_input_1$z_vec ), data = list( y = data_input_1$y ), inits = inits, dimensions = list( beta_tr = c(K_val, 2), beta = c(K_val, 2), k_vec = c(N_val) ), buildDerivs = TRUE ) #> Defining model #> Building model #> Setting data and initial values #> Running calculate on model #> [Note] Any error reports that follow may simply reflect missing values in model variables. #> Checking model sizes and dimensions # Compile Cmodel <- compileNimble(model) #> Compiling #> [Note] This may take a minute. #> [Note] Use 'showCompilerOutput = TRUE' to see C++ compilation details. # Key step: Configure HMC conf <- configureHMC(model) #> ===== Monitors ===== #> thin = 1: beta_tr, mu0, mu1, sigma0, sigma1 #> ===== Samplers ===== #> NUTS sampler (1) #> - beta_tr[1, 1], beta_tr[2, 1], beta_tr[1, 2], beta_tr[2, 2], sigma0, sigma1, mu0, mu1 conf$addMonitors(c("mu0", "mu1", "sigma0", "sigma1", "beta_tr", "beta")) #> thin = 1: beta, beta_tr, mu0, mu1, sigma0, sigma1 conf$printSamplers() #> [1] NUTS sampler: beta_tr[1, 1], beta_tr[2, 1], beta_tr[1, 2], beta_tr[2, 2], sigma0, sigma1, mu0, mu1 # Build and compile HMC sampler Rmcmc <- buildMCMC(conf) Cmcmc <- compileNimble(Rmcmc, project = model) #> Compiling #> [Note] This may take a minute. #> [Note] Use 'showCompilerOutput = TRUE' to see C++ compilation details. # Run the sampler samples <- runMCMC( Cmcmc, niter = 20000, nburnin = 5000, thin = 10, nchains = 4, samplesAsCodaMCMC = TRUE ) #> running chain 1... #> [Note] NUTS sampler (nodes: beta_tr[1, 1], beta_tr[2, 1], beta_tr[1, 2], beta_tr[2, 2], sigma0, sigma1, mu0, mu1) is using 5000 warmup iterations. #> Since `warmupMode` is 'default' and `nburnin` > 0, #> the number of warmup iterations is equal to `nburnin`. #> The burnin samples will be discarded, and all samples returned will be post-warmup. #> |-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------| #> |-------------------------------------------------------| #> running chain 2... #> |-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------| #> |-------------------------------------------------------| #> running chain 3... #> |-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------| #> |-------------------------------------------------------| #> running chain 4... #> |-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------| #> |-------------------------------------------------------| # Diagnosis library(coda) #plot(samples) summary(samples) #> #> Iterations = 1:1500 #> Thinning interval = 1 #> Number of chains = 4 #> Sample size per chain = 1500 #> #> 1. Empirical mean and standard deviation for each variable, #> plus standard error of the mean: #> #> Mean SD Naive SE Time-series SE #> beta[1, 1] -0.96527 0.2837 0.003662 0.003663 #> beta[2, 1] -0.83980 0.2778 0.003586 0.003642 #> beta[1, 2] 0.83622 0.3836 0.004953 0.004954 #> beta[2, 2] 0.55057 0.3766 0.004862 0.005051 #> beta_tr[1, 1] -0.15971 0.8018 0.010352 0.010678 #> beta_tr[2, 1] 0.03576 0.7961 0.010278 0.010935 #> beta_tr[1, 2] 0.21680 0.7940 0.010250 0.010784 #> beta_tr[2, 2] -0.13867 0.7997 0.010324 0.011057 #> mu0 -0.82547 0.7589 0.009797 0.010346 #> mu1 0.64073 0.8046 0.010388 0.010557 #> sigma0 0.85711 0.7673 0.009906 0.009908 #> sigma1 0.96021 0.7897 0.010195 0.009941 #> #> 2. Upper C.I. #> beta[1, 1] 1 1 #> beta[2, 1] 1 1 #> beta[1, 2] 1 1 #> beta[2, 2] 1 1 #> beta_tr[1, 1] 1 1 #> beta_tr[2, 1] 1 1 #> beta_tr[1, 2] 1 1 #> beta_tr[2, 2] 1 1 #> mu0 1 1 #> mu1 1 1 #> sigma0 1 1 #> sigma1 1 1 #> #> Multivariate psrf #> #> 1 effectiveSize(samples) #> beta[1, 1] beta[2, 1] beta[1, 2] beta[2, 2] beta_tr[1, 1] #> 6000.000 5836.280 6000.000 5642.100 5760.170 #> beta_tr[2, 1] beta_tr[1, 2] beta_tr[2, 2] mu0 mu1 #> 5555.880 5548.970 5333.085 5505.775 5825.739 #> sigma0 sigma1 #> 6000.000 6329.500 all_samples <- do.call(rbind, samples) beta_nimble2 <- all_samples[, "beta[1, 1]"] ``` ::: ### Plot and compare ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df <- data.frame( value = c(beta_stan, beta_jags, beta_nimble1, beta_nimble2), source = factor(rep( c("Stan", "JAGS", "NIMBLE", "NIMBLE - HMC"), times = c(length(beta_stan), length(beta_jags), length(beta_nimble1), length(beta_nimble2)) )) ) library(ggplot2) ggplot(df, aes(x = value, color = source)) + geom_density(size = 1) + labs(title = "Posterior density of beta[1,1]", x = "beta[1,1]", y = "Density") + theme_minimal() #> Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0. #> ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead. ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](stan_jags_nimble2_files/figure-html/plot_res-1.png){width=672} ::: :::
